Skip to main content
30-Day C++ Sprint Repository
Day 3 of 30

Mission Brief

What

A 30-day, project-based C++ curriculum. One program per day. Each builds on the previous. By Day 30: a multi-file, class-based Student Management System using vectors, algorithms, I/O validation, and clean architecture.

Target: VEX V5 robotics codebase contribution. The C++ used here maps directly to PROS / VEXcode: tasks, controllers, motor groups, PID loops, state machines.

Why

Tutorial hell produces passive recognition. This sprint forces active retrieval: you write from memory, diff against a reference, annotate gaps. The commit log becomes the proof of work — not a certificate, not a streak, a history of decisions and corrections.

Different How

  • Solved/Blank pattern: Every day has a solved/ (reference) and blank/ (your attempt). You study solved, close it, write blank.
  • Retrieval test: The diff is the test. No quizzes. No multiple choice.
  • Honest log: Commits show retries, bugs, regressions. day-06: Debugging Challenge — 5 bugs injected, 5 fixed, notes attached.
  • PID integration (Days 28–30): Not an afterthought. Brian Douglas's intuition-first control theory. P → PI → PID on a simulated plant. Directly applicable to VEX drivetrain / lift control.

Scope

C++17. Standard library only. No frameworks. No external deps except the PID video series. Compiler: GCC/Clang. Build: CMake (introduced Day 5). Editor: your choice.

Goals

Fluency without reference

Write Day 1–15 programs from memory. No syntax lookup. Variables, loops, functions, arrays — automatic.

Multi-file comfort

Organize code across .h/.cpp pairs. CMake builds. Header guards. Forward declarations. Link-time thinking.

Debugging instinct

Day 6: inject 5 bug classes. Fix all. Log why each happened. Build the mental catalog: "this symptom → that cause."

STL as default

Vectors over arrays. Algorithms over raw loops. Iterators over indices. std::string over char*. RAII everywhere.

Class design discipline

Private data. Constructors enforce invariants. Getters only when needed. No anemic structs with logic outside.

PID intuition

Explain P/PI/PID behavior on a plant without equations. Tune by feel. Recognize overshoot, steady-state error, oscillation.

Retrieval habit

Study solved → close → write blank → diff → annotate. Every day. The diff is the test. The log is the grade.

Ship Day 30

Student Management System: menu-driven, multi-file, validated I/O, vector storage, class hierarchy, search/sort/stats. Clean enough to hand to a teammate.

Architecture

Solved / Blank Pattern

Each day has two directories:

day-XX/
├── solved/     # Reference implementation
│   ├── main.cpp
│   ├── *.h
│   └── *.cpp
└── blank/      # Your attempt (write from memory)
    ├── main.cpp
    └── *.h / *.cpp (as needed)

Workflow: Study solved/ → Close it → Implement in blank/ → Diff → Annotate gaps.

Retrieval Testing Loop

Study solved/ (15–30 min) │ ▼ Close all references │ ▼ Write blank/ from memory │ ▼ Diff: solved/ vs blank/ │ ├─► Missing? → Annotate: "forgot bounds check" ├─► Wrong? → Annotate: "off-by-one in loop" ├─► Better? → Annotate: "used algorithm, cleaner" └─► Style? → Annotate: "naming inconsistent" │ ▼ Commit both + annotations │ ▼ Next day

Why This Works

  • Active recall > passive re-reading. The struggle to reconstruct strengthens memory traces.
  • Immediate feedback from the diff — no waiting for a grader.
  • Metacognition — annotation forces you to name the gap: "I forgot to validate input" vs "I didn't know."
  • Artifact trail — the commit log shows progression, not just final state. Retry commits are visible.

PID Integration (Days 28–30)

Resource

Brian Douglas — "Control Systems Lectures" (PID Playlist)

youtube.com/playlist?list=PLn8PRpmsu08pQBgjxYFXSsODEF3Jqmm-y

Watch: PID Control Intro, PID Tuning, PID Implementation. ~45 minutes total.

Why Intuition-First

  • Equations-first teaches symbol manipulation. Intuition-first teaches behavior prediction.
  • On a VEX robot: you feel overshoot, you see steady-state error, you hear oscillation. The equations map to those sensations.
  • Brian Douglas builds mental models: "P is spring, I is accumulated memory, D is damper." That transfers to tuning.
  • Day 29–30: you implement PID on a simulated plant (mass-spring-damper), then tune by feel. No Ziegler-Nichols memorization.

Day 28 — P Controller

  • Simulate 1D mass with force input
  • Implement P-only: u = Kp * error
  • Observe: steady-state error, oscillation vs Kp
  • Log: Kp values tested, resulting behavior

Day 29 — PI Controller

  • Add integral term: integral += error * dt
  • Anti-windup: clamp integral
  • Observe: zero steady-state error, overshoot trade-off
  • Log: Ki values, settling time, overshoot

Day 30 — Full PID + Final Project

  • Add derivative: derivative = (error - prevError) / dt
  • Noise handling: low-pass filter on D
  • Tune all three gains by feel
  • Integrate into Student Management System as "Grade PID Simulator" module (optional stretch)

VEX Transfer

  • Drivetrain distance PID → position control
  • Lift height PID → hold position against gravity
  • Flywheel velocity PID → consistent RPM
  • Same structure, different plants. The tuning mindset transfers.

Daily Curriculum

2 complete · Day 3 current · 27 remaining

Day Project Core Concept Status Action
1 Temperature Converter Functions, variables, I/O, basic arithmetic ✓
2 Rectangle Calculator Multiple variables, function parameters, area/perimeter ✓
3 Shopping Calculator Expressions, operator precedence, rounding ●
4 Basic Calculator Multiple small functions, if/else, operator handling ○
5 Multi-file Calculator Header files, multiple source files, project organization ○
6 Debugging Challenge Syntax, runtime, logical errors, debugging workflow ○
7 BMI Calculator Data types: integers, floating point, bool, char ○
8 Username Validator Strings, conditions, validation rules ○
9 Movie Ticket System if/else statements, age-based pricing ○
10 Rock Paper Scissors Decision making, nested if/else, switch ○
11 Guess the Number while loops, loop control ○
12 Multiplication Table for loops, iteration ○
13 Pattern Generator Nested loops, pattern printing ○
14 FizzBuzz Loops with conditions, modulo operator ○
15 Dice Simulator Random numbers, distributions, statistics ○
16 Function Overloading Function overloading, default arguments ○
17 Array Statistics Arrays, traversal, aggregate operations ○
18 Reverse Array Array manipulation, in-place algorithms ○
19 Pointer Swap Pointers, pass-by-pointer, dereferencing ○
20 Pointer Traversal Pointer arithmetic, array traversal without indexing ○
21 References References vs pointers, when to use each ○
22 Student Record System Structs, structured data, formatted output ○
23 Restaurant Ordering System Enums, type aliases, menu-driven programs ○
24 Project Refactor (Scope & Linkage) Scope, duration, linkage, file organization ○
25 Password Strength Checker std::string advanced, character classification ○
26 Gradebook (Vectors) std::vector, dynamic containers, push_back ○
27 Search & Sort Iterators, algorithms, std::find, std::sort ○
28 Bank Account Class Classes, member functions, encapsulation basics ○
29 Enhanced Bank Account Constructors, private members, getters, validation, vector of objects ○
30 Student Management System (Final Project) Comprehensive review: menus, functions, vectors, classes, pointers/references, input validation, multi-file organization ○

Daily Curriculum

2 complete · Day 3 current · 27 remaining

Day 1

Temperature Converter

✓
Complete

Core Concept

Functions, variables, I/O, basic arithmetic

Description

Welcome the user. Ask for a temperature in Celsius. Convert it to Fahrenheit. Display the result. Skills: main(), variables, user input, return values, function parameters, basic arithmetic.

Requirements

Day 2

Rectangle Calculator

✓
Complete

Core Concept

Multiple variables, function parameters, area/perimeter

Description

Ask for width and height. Calculate area and perimeter. Display both neatly. Extra: Add decimal support.

Requirements

Extra Challenge

Add decimal support

Day 3

Shopping Calculator

●
In Progress

Core Concept

Expressions, operator precedence, rounding

Description

Input price, quantity, tax percentage. Calculate subtotal, tax amount, total price. Extra: Round to two decimal places.

Requirements

Extra Challenge

Round to two decimal places

Day 4

Basic Calculator

○
Pending

Core Concept

Multiple small functions, if/else, operator handling

Description

Input number, operator, number. Supported operators: +, -, *, /. Display result. Inside calculate() use if/else. Extra: Handle division by zero.

Requirements

Extra Challenge

Handle division by zero

Day 5

Multi-file Calculator

○
Pending

Core Concept

Header files, multiple source files, project organization

Description

Split calculator into main.cpp, calculator.cpp, calculator.h. main() should only call functions. calculator.h contains declarations, calculator.cpp contains definitions.

Requirements

Day 6

Debugging Challenge

○
Pending

Core Concept

Syntax, runtime, logical errors, debugging workflow

Description

Take your calculator. Intentionally create: syntax error, logical error, runtime error, incorrect output, missing return. Fix every one. Keep notes explaining why it happened and how you fixed it.

Requirements

Day 7

BMI Calculator

○
Pending

Core Concept

Data types: integers, floating point, bool, char

Description

Input weight (kg) and height (m). Calculate BMI. Display category: Underweight, Healthy, Overweight, Obese.

Requirements

Day 8

Username Validator

○
Pending

Core Concept

Strings, conditions, validation rules

Description

Ask for username. Rules: minimum length, maximum length, first letter alphabetic, no spaces. Display every failed rule.

Requirements

Day 9

Movie Ticket System

○
Pending

Core Concept

if/else statements, age-based pricing

Description

Ask for age. Determine ticket price: child/adult/senior pricing. Bonus: Student discount.

Requirements

Extra Challenge

Add student discount

Day 10

Rock Paper Scissors

○
Pending

Core Concept

Decision making, nested if/else, switch

Description

Input: Rock, Paper, Scissors. Computer choice is hardcoded. Determine winner.

Requirements

Day 11

Guess the Number

○
Pending

Core Concept

while loops, loop control

Description

Computer stores 42. Keep asking until guessed. Display: Too high, Too low, Correct. Count guesses.

Requirements

Day 12

Multiplication Table

○
Pending

Core Concept

for loops, iteration

Description

Input a number (e.g., 7). Output multiplication table up to 12. Bonus: Let user choose ending number.

Requirements

Extra Challenge

Let user choose ending number

Day 13

Pattern Generator

○
Pending

Core Concept

Nested loops, pattern printing

Description

Print triangle patterns: left-aligned, right-aligned, pyramid. Uses nested loops.

Requirements

Day 14

FizzBuzz

○
Pending

Core Concept

Loops with conditions, modulo operator

Description

For numbers 1–100: Fizz for multiples of 3, Buzz for 5, FizzBuzz for both, number otherwise. Bonus: User chooses ending number.

Requirements

Extra Challenge

Allow user to choose ending number

Day 15

Dice Simulator

○
Pending

Core Concept

Random numbers, distributions, statistics

Description

Roll two dice 100 times. Display every roll, total, average, highest roll, lowest roll.

Requirements

Day 16

Function Overloading

○
Pending

Core Concept

Function overloading, default arguments

Description

Create add(), multiply(), print() with multiple versions: add(int,int), add(double,double), add(int,int,int), etc.

Requirements

Day 17

Array Statistics

○
Pending

Core Concept

Arrays, traversal, aggregate operations

Description

Input 10 numbers. Display largest, smallest, average, sum. Bonus: Count even numbers.

Requirements

Extra Challenge

Count even numbers

Day 18

Reverse Array

○
Pending

Core Concept

Array manipulation, in-place algorithms

Description

Store 10 values. Reverse them manually without std::reverse().

Requirements

Day 19

Pointer Swap

○
Pending

Core Concept

Pointers, pass-by-pointer, dereferencing

Description

Input a and b. Swap using pointers. Print before and after. No return values.

Requirements

Day 20

Pointer Traversal

○
Pending

Core Concept

Pointer arithmetic, array traversal without indexing

Description

Store an array. Traverse using only pointer arithmetic. Find largest, smallest, average. No array[i] inside traversal function.

Requirements

Day 21

References

○
Pending

Core Concept

References vs pointers, when to use each

Description

Rewrite Day 19 swap using references. Write comparison: when to choose pointer vs reference.

Requirements

Day 22

Student Record System

○
Pending

Core Concept

Structs, structured data, formatted output

Description

Each student has: name, age, grade. Allow user to enter 5 students, then display all records in a formatted table.

Day 23

Restaurant Ordering System

Pending

Core Concept

Enums, type aliases, menu-driven programs

Description

Create menu with numbered items (using enum). Let user choose items, calculate total bill, print itemized receipt.

Day 24

Project Refactor (Scope & Linkage)

○
Pending

Core Concept

Scope, duration, linkage, file organization

Description

Take Student Record or Restaurant system and reorganize: move reusable functions to separate files, eliminate unnecessary globals, keep variables in smallest possible scope.

Requirements

Day 25

Password Strength Checker

○
Pending

Core Concept

std::string advanced, character classification

Description

Evaluate password: minimum length, uppercase, lowercase, number, special character. Display which requirements passed and overall strength rating.

Day 26

Gradebook (Vectors)

Pending

Core Concept

std::vector, dynamic containers, push_back

Description

Allow user to enter unknown number of grades (until they stop). Display: all grades, highest, lowest, average.

Day 27

Search & Sort

Pending

Core Concept

Iterators, algorithms, std::find, std::sort

Description

Extend Gradebook: search for a grade, sort ascending, sort descending, remove a grade. Use standard algorithms where appropriate.

Day 28

Bank Account Class

Pending

Core Concept

Classes, member functions, encapsulation basics

Description

Implement BankAccount class: account holder name, balance, deposit, withdraw, display balance. Instantiate multiple accounts in main().

Day 29

Enhanced Bank Account

○
Pending

Core Concept

Constructors, private members, getters, validation, vector of objects

Description

Improve Bank Account: constructors, private data members, getter functions, input validation (no negative deposits), multiple accounts in std::vector.

Day 30

Student Management System (Final Project)

○
Pending

Core Concept

Comprehensive review: menus, functions, vectors, classes, pointers/references, input validation, multi-file organization

Description

Menu-driven system: Add student, Remove student, Search by name, Display all students, Display class statistics (highest, lowest, average), Exit. Multi-file, clean organization, validated input.

Requirements

Extra Challenge

Organize across multiple .cpp/.h files; validate all input

Review Process

Phase 1: Build

Write the day's program in blank/. No reference to solved/. Compile. Fix syntax errors. Run. Fix runtime errors. Test edge cases.

Phase 2: Test

Verify every requirement from the spec. Add ad-hoc tests for edge cases not in requirements. Document any behavior you're unsure about.

Phase 3: Retrieve

Diff blank/ against solved/. For every difference: categorize (missing, wrong, better, style). Annotate in commit message or separate notes file.

Phase 4: Log

Commit both directories. Message: day-XX: Project Name — status. Push. The log is the artifact. Future you reads it to reconstruct reasoning.

Commit Message Convention

day-07: BMI Calculator — fixed float comparison bug
day-14: FizzBuzz — added user-defined upper bound
day-22: Student Record — refactored to separate files
day-28: PID Plant — P-only controller, steady-state error logged

Status keywords: done, wip, bugfix, refactor, retry. No "feat", "fix" — the day number is the scope.

Repository · Eleventy · Admin
